summary: Gruber reflects on WWDC 2009 as a turning point where the iPhone emerged as a peer platform to the Mac in Apple's developer ecosystem. He contrasts the vitality of WWDC with the decline of Sun's JavaOne conference, using it as a reminder that industry fortunes shift. The conference's evolution is captured by a session titled 'Mac Programming for iPhone Developers,' inverting the expected hierarchy. Gruber argues the iPhone will supplant the Mac as Apple's primary platform within a year, noting that iPhone OS and Mac OS X user bases are roughly equal. He also catches Apple using a misleading chart that combined Mac and iPhone users under the label 'OS X.' The piece positions 2009 as the inflection point where Apple's center of gravity began shifting decisively toward mobile.
key_points: WWDC's transformation from a Mac-only conference to a dual-platform event happened in just two years, with iPhone sessions drawing equal or greater interest
The session 'Mac Programming for iPhone Developers' perfectly encapsulated the reversal of the expected platform hierarchy
Apple's 'OS X' user graph misleadingly combined Mac and iPhone users to show 75 million, when in reality each platform had roughly 35-40 million
Sun's JavaOne conference, held the week before at the same venue, served as a stark reminder that even massive developer ecosystems can decline
Apple renamed its technical keynote from 'Mac OS X State of the Union' to 'Core OS State of the Union,' signaling the platforms' symbiotic convergence
money_quotes: Across the street from Moscone West on the first day of WWDC, there remained banners from Sun's JavaOne conference the week prior — a touching reminder that times and fortunes change, and that annual conferences often serve as markers of the state of the industry.
To crudely paraphrase Dylan, a conference not busy being born is busy dying. WWDC is busy being born.
This year, the emblematic session was titled 'Mac Programming for iPhone Developers'. I'm not even sure what to say about that.

Apple now has two full-fledged developer platforms, Mac OS X and iPhone OS, derived from one core system. Neither felt more important than the other this year at WWDC, which is remarkable considering that one of them hadn't even shipped two years ago.
I think it will have taken just three years for the iPhone to supplant the Mac as Apple's primary platform. By 2011 it will be obvious.
It's simply a matter of users.
It was a very misleading and poorly-designed chart.
key_insight: WWDC 2009 marked the moment the iPhone achieved parity with the Mac in Apple's ecosystem, and the growth trajectories made it clear the iPhone would soon become Apple's primary platform.
